videos.transcript: What's up guys! We're here on Langkawi Island on Malaysia. In this video, we're going to show you the best things to do here, including this really unique bridge behind us. So hope you enjoy it! The Langkawi Archipelago is located off the west coast of mainland Malaysia. This cluster in the Andaman sea is made up of 99 islands and only four are inhabited. The main Langkawi island is accessible by plane or ferry, and it's the most popular one to visit. It's also the largest island and has a ton to offer. From its stunning beaches to its glorious waterfalls, viewpoints, jungle-clad hills, and mangroves, there's so much beauty here. In this video, we'll highlight 10 of the best things to do on Langkawi Island. Now let's dive in! We're Zac and Ine. We make helpful videos about destinations around the world so you can easily plan your own adventures. Located at the northern coastline, Sandy Skulls Beach is a true local favorite. It's a beautiful secluded beach with soft white sands, shallow waters for swimming, and forested trees for shade. The bay is small but it's also really peaceful, with few crowds. When you're in the area, it's also worth making the five minute drive to nearby the Temurun Falls, the tallest waterfall on the island. A short and gradual hiking trail leads to the rocky cliff. When the night falls, the markets open their doors. You can find a market at any day of the week on Langkawi, but the location changes each day. We put a list on the screen here with the different market options for you. It's a really great way to try some traditional Malaysian food and mingle with the island locals. We visited the Temoyong Night Market near Cenang Beach on Thursday. This market offered lots of juices, murtabak, nasi lemak, and much more. Cenang Beach is the most popular and beloved beach on the island, with lots of activities and tourist facilities right at your fingertips. Try jet skiing or paragliding, or simply relax at a beach bar, it's all possible at this long stretch of white sand beach. We highly recommend watching the sunset here and catching a beautiful light show after the sun goes down. Cenang is also a great place to stay during your visit to the island as it's located in the middle of all the other sight and activities. We left a link to the hotel we stayed at in the description below the video. Another fun thing to do in Cenang is to visit the rice fields. Here, you'll find vast stretches of beautiful paddy fields, and if you're lucky, you might spot water buffaloes. At the time of our visit, it was free to enter. If you come to the rice paddies, it's also worth to check out the small museum because they have a lot of explanations about the cultivation of rice. One of the most popular activities on Langkawi is to take a cable car to the top of Machincang Mountain. From the base, you'll ascend to the Middle Station, covering about 1700 meters or 5,577 feet in distance. Spend some time at the viewing platforms here before riding the cable car to the Top Station. The summit sits at an elevation of 708 meters or 2, 322 feet, providing spectacular views of the surrounding islands and rainforests. There's also a suspension bridge that you can access for a small fee. The cable car leaves from the Oriental Village, but be aware that there might be long lines. It's best to come early or later in the day, but you'll need at least three to four hours to do everything on the mountain. A fun thing to do with kids or as a couple in the Oriental Village is to visit the 3D Art In Paradise Museum. The museum houses over 100 different three-dimensional artworks that are divided up into nine zones and themes. From an Aquarium Zone to a Safari Zone and a Glow-in-the-Dark Zone, you can step inside the exhibits and become the subject of the artwork. It's a great activity on a rainy day or to combine with a visit to the cable car. Near the Oriental Village you'll find the Seven Wells Waterfall, which are widely considered to be the most impressive falls on Langkawi. Its name comes from seven natural pools or wells that you can find in the rock face. To get here, you can hike up stairs through a rainforest. The trail is short but steep and takes you to the lower falls first. Then, you can continue to the top of the waterfalls where you'll find pools to cool off. The seven wells pools is actually a natural little water slide so I'm gonna give it a go. Langkawi's northeast coastline is covered with a dense mangrove forest, which has been protected under UNESCO since 2007. Officially named Kilim Geoforest Park, this natural area can only be visited by taking a boat tour. Most tours last two to three hours and take you around the swamp, limestone formations, rugged caves, and areas of the forested lands. You can also encounter wildlife, such as eagles, snakes, and monkeys. But be careful when selecting a boat tour because there are many unethical organizations that feed the eagles to attract them. We opted for an ethical tour during which we learned so much from our guide about the importance of the mangroves. One of the most scenic beaches in Langkawi is called Tanjung Rhu Beach, which lies at the edge of the Geoforest Park in the north of the island. Due to its slightly hidden location, you'll find clean white sands and very few crowds here. We had it almost all to ourselves, making this our favorite beach on the island. Finally, if you have some spare time it's worth driving up to the island's tallest peak. Gunung Raya sits at 881 meters or 2,890 feet and offers beautiful views of the island and the sea. But even better than the view is the actual drive itself. Winding through the primary forest, you can spot many animals here. We encountered hornbills, macaque monkeys, eagles, and many cute dusky leaf monkeys. It's best to rent a scooter from town to get here, but be careful as the road is steep in parts and many stray dogs wander around. There are a few things to do on Langkawi that we didn't mention and we've included a list of those on the screen here for you. If you're planning your Malaysia trip, we recommend keeping things organized with our interactive travel planner. Find a discount code below. We also included important links that you'll need for your Langkawi trip in the description, such as an interactive map so you can get to each place that we mentioned. We'll catch you on the next adventure.

videos.title: 🇲🇾 14 Best Places To Visit In LANGKAWI! | Malaysia Travel Guide
videos.description:
Discover the 14 best things to do in Langkawi Malaysia with our Langkawi Travel Guide 2026! 🇲🇾 ✨
Planning your 2026 trip to Langkawi Island?🌴✨
Here you will find out what to do in Langkawi, as we explore the 14 best places to visit in Langkawi, from the iconic Langkawi Sky Bridge and scenic Cable Car ride to the natural wonders of Kilim Karst Geoforest Park and Telaga Tujuh Waterfall. Relax on Langkawi's best beaches like Pantai Cenang and Tanjung Rhu, explore cultural gems like Oriental Village and Kampung Lubok Buaya, and enjoy family fun at Underwater World Langkawi.

We will also take you off the beaten path to Dayang Bunting Marble Geoforest Park and snorkeling paradise at Pulau Payar Marine Park to make your Langkawi trip even better.

Explore Langkawi's diverse must-see attractions, savor its local flavors and enjoy a memorable tropical getaway.

Don't forget to like, and subscribe for more travel tips and guides. 
https://www.youtube.com/@TravelTipsDestinations?sub_confirmation=1  

#langkawi  #malaysiatravelguide #langkawiisland 

Discover other destinations in Malaysia:
https://youtu.be/a5PpetfnmaY
https://youtu.be/HnfRsCz_wjs
https://youtu.be/XUPU5QGH4f0

14 Top Things to do in Langkawi, Malaysia (2026 Travel Guide):
1. Langkawi Sky Bridge: Walk across this curved pedestrian bridge suspended 660 meters above sea level for panoramic views.
2. Langkawi Cable Car (SkyCab): Ascend Mount Mat Cincang via the world's steepest cable car, offering breathtaking vistas.
3. Kilim Karst Geoforest Park: Explore mangroves, limestone cliffs, and bat caves in this UNESCO-listed geopark.
4. Telaga Tujuh (Seven Wells Waterfall): Hike to this stunning waterfall with seven natural pools amidst lush rainforest.
5. Pantai Cenang (Cenang Beach): Relax on Langkawi's most popular beach, known for its white sands and vibrant atmosphere.
6. Dataran Lang (Eagle Square): Visit the iconic 12-meter eagle sculpture overlooking the harbor.
7. Pantai Tengah (Tengah Beach): Enjoy a quieter beach experience with soft sands and a laid-back vibe.
8. Kampung Lubok Buaya: Experience traditional Malay village life and savor local street food.
9. Pantai Tanjung Rhu (Tanjung Rhu Beach): Discover pristine sands and clear waters at this secluded beach.
10. MAHA Tower: Take in sweeping views from this sleek observation tower near Kuah waterfront.
11. Underwater World Langkawi: Explore one of Southeast Asia's largest aquariums, home to diverse marine life.
12. Langkawi Oriental Village: Explore this open-air complex designed like a traditional Asian village, featuring shops, eateries, and cultural exhibits.
13. Dayang Bunting Marble Geoforest Park: Visit the legendary Lake of the Pregnant Maiden, surrounded by limestone cliffs and lush jungle.
14. Snorkeling in Pulau Payar Marine Park: Snorkel in this protected reef teeming with coral gardens and colorful fish. Pulau Beras Basah is an alternative, when Pulau Payar is closed for conservation from March 1 to May 31. 

4 Must-Try Local Langkawi Dishes:
Laksa Kedah: Thick rice noodles in a spicy fish-based broth flavored with tamarind and herbs.
Ikan Bakar: Grilled fish marinated in spices, served with sambal, rice, and fresh greens.
Kerabu Rice: Blue rice mixed with fresh herbs, coconut, lemongrass, and shredded fish.
Apam Balik: Crispy folded pancake filled with crushed peanuts, sugar, and corn or banana.

5 Essential Travel Tips:
1. Best Time to Visit: November to March offers clear skies and calm seas, ideal for outdoor activities.
2. Getting Around: Rent a scooter or car, or use ride-hailing apps like Grab for convenient travel.
3. Mosquito Protection: Pack insect repellent and wear long sleeves, especially around dusk.
4. Jellyfish Awareness: Be cautious between May and October; consider protective swimwear or hotel pools.
5. Cultural Respect: Dress modestly in villages and mosques; alcohol is available but consume respectfully.

videos.transcript: Welcome to Lancawi, a tropical paradise of emerald waters, rainforest cloaked hills, and white sandy beaches. In this 11-minute travel guide, we will explore the 14 top attractions in Lancawi, four delicious local dishes, and five essential travel tips to help you make the most of your island getaway. Lancawi is a breathtaking archipelago of 99 islands in the Andaman Sea just off the northwestern coast of Malaysia where Lancawi Island is the largest and most developed. From the iconic Lancawi Skybridge and the lively shores of Pantanang to the tranquil Dang Bunting Lake, Lancawi offers a perfect mix of adventure, natural beauty, and relaxation. Let's dive in and discover the top 14 things to do in Lancawi. The first 12 attractions are located on Lancawi Island, the main island of the archipelago, renowned for its duty-free status and its rich combination of lush landscapes and thrilling experiences. The first attraction is the Lancawi Skybridge. Step onto one of the world's most spectacular curved pedestrian bridges suspended 660 m above sea level. To reach the sky bridge, take the sky cab up Mount Matsin Kang. Then either ride the glass enclosed skylide or hike about 15 minutes to the bridge. From there, enjoy sweeping views of lush mountains and the turquoise Anderman Sea. As you walk across, the sky bridge sways gently with the breeze, adding a thrill to the breathtaking scenery. Next up at number two is the Lancawi cable car, known as the Sky Cab. Ascend Mount Matsin Kang in a thrilling cable car ride that soarses above rainforest canopies and cascading waterfalls. The world's steepest cable car rewards you with awe inspiring vistas from several viewing platforms. At the top, you'll also find a cafe with panoramic seating and access to the sky trail for those eager to hike deeper into the landscape. Coming in at number three is the Kllumcast Geo Forest Park. Explore Lancawi's UNESCO listed geo park by boat as you weave through winding mangroves, dramatic limestone cliffs, and echoing batcaves. This eco tour also includes eagle feeding zones and floating fish farms, offering a rich glimpse into the island's wildlife and geological heritage. At number four, we have Talaga Tuju, the Seven Wells Waterfall. Hike up to a spectacular natural cascade that flows through seven interconnected pools on the slopes of Mount Matt Sinc. The jungle trail is shaded and scenic with small swimming spots where you can cool off and take a break. According to local legend, the upper pools are believed to be home to forest fairies, adding a touch of enchantment to your trek. Number five is Pante Chennang or Chennang Beach. Relax on Lancawi's most popular beach with soft white sand and swaying palms. This lively hot spot is the island's tourism hub with beachside cafes, shops, jet ski rentals, and bars offering colorful cocktails at sunset. You can even enjoy a massage in an open air cabana while listening to the waves roll in. Next up at number six is Daran Lang or the Eagle Square. Snap a selfie with Lancawi's spectacular 12 meter eagle sculpture poised dramatically for flight. This landmark is the symbol of the Lancawi Island and it overlooks the harbor where fairies and boats come and go. As night falls the surrounding fountains light up making it a serene spot for an evening stroll. Coming in at number seven is Panti Tangga or Tangga Beach. Just south of Shenang, this quieter beach is ideal for those seeking tranquility. Its laid-back vibe, soft sands, and uncrowded shoreline make it perfect for romantic sunset walks or quiet sunbathing. The area also features boutique resorts and yoga studios catering to a more mindful, wellness focused traveler. At number eight, we have Kong Lubok Buaya. Explore authentic village life just a short distance from the tourist buzz of Panti Chanang. This quiet community gives visitors a glimpse of traditional Malay homes, slower rhythms of life, and local culture. Don't miss the roadside stalls where you can sample street food favorites like sate and a palm balik. Number nine on our list is Panti Tanjong Ru or Tanjong Ru Beach. One of Lancawi's most pristine and secluded stretches of sand. Tanjong Ru offers powdery beaches, shallow, crystal clearar waters, and majestic rock formations rising from the sea. You can also hire a boat from here to explore hidden coes and limestone caves nearby, perfect for those craving solitude and natural beauty. Next up at number 10 is the Maha Tower. This sleek addition to Lancawi's skyline stands proudly near the Kua waterfront, offering sweeping views from its observation deck. While it's an excellent daytime destination, the tower truly comes alive at sunset and in the evening when its exterior lights transform it into a glowing beacon. Number 11 is Underwater World Lancawi. One of Southeast Asia's largest aquariums, this familyfriendly spot is home to over 200 marine species, including sharks, stingrays, and even playful penguins. The highlight is a walkthrough tunnel that immerses you in an underwater world. And the daily penguin feeding sessions are a hit with kids and adults alike. At number 12, we have the Oriental Village at the base of the Lancawi cable car. This open air complex styled like a traditional Asian village blends local Malay and East Asian architecture. And it is the perfect launchpad for your Mount Matt Sinkang adventure. Explore the villages lively mix of shops, eeries, cultural exhibits, and familyfriendly attractions all in one scenic setting. And with that, we wrap up Lancawi Island's top 12 attractions. Now, let's venture beyond the main island to explore two more must visit spots in the Lancawi Archipelago. Attraction number 13 is Dang Bunting Marble Geo Forest Park. Just south of Lancawi lies this UNESCO listed gem with the legendary Lake of the Pregnant Maiden. A mystical freshwater lake framed by limestone cliffs and lush jungle. The lakes's name comes from the surrounding hills, said to resemble a reclining pregnant woman and is believed to boost fertility. The Geoforest Park also features dramatic marble cliffs, jungle trails, hidden caves, and rare wildlife. Hike, swim, or kayak as you explore one of Lancawi's most famous and geologically fascinating sites. To get there, join a guided island hopping tour or hire a boat from Panti Chanang or Kua Jetti. It is a 15-minute ride across the sea. And last but not least, we have snorkeling at Pula Paya Marine Park. Dive into Lancawi's top marine sanctuary, a protected reef teameming with coral gardens, colorful fish, and baby reef sharks. Snorkel in crystal clearar shallow waters about 30 km south of Lancawi Island. Reachable by catamaran or speedboat from Kua Jetti. Note that the park is closed annually from the 1st of March to 31st of May for reef conservation, so plan your visit accordingly. If you're visiting during this period, consider a guided snorkeling trip to Pula Berisbasa, closer to Lancawi, offering calm waters with plenty of marine life, especially near the reefs. Lancawi may not be as famous as Paneang for street food, but it still boasts delicious dishes deeply rooted in Malay and island traditions. Here are four traditional dishes to try when visiting Lancawi. Laxa is Lancawi's version of Laxa. Made with thick rice noodles in a spicy fishbased broth flavored with tamarind and herbs. Ikan baka is a flavorful grilled fish marinated in spices and cooked over hot coals. Typically served with sambble, rice, and fresh greens. Kurabu rice is a colorful northern Malaysian dish that mixes blue rice dyed with butterfly pea flour with fresh herbs, coconut, lemongrass, and shredded fish. And finally, don't miss a panalik, a crispy folded pancake filled with crushed peanuts, sugar, and corn or banana. A sweet treat popular in Lancawi's night markets. To make the most of your visit to Lancawi, being wellprepared with practical information is key. Here are five travel tips to help you enjoy the island and make the most of your stay. The ideal time to visit Lancawi is during the dry season between November and March. This is when skies are clear, seas are calm, and outdoor activities are most enjoyable. With no public transport system, the best way to explore Lancawi is by renting a scooter or a car. Alternatively, use a ride hailing app like Grab for convenient and affordable rides across the island. While Lancawi is a tropical paradise, mosquitoes can be common, particularly in jungle areas and around dusk. Bring or buy insect repellent and wear long sleeves in the evening if you are heading inland. Jellyfish are more common on Lancawi in July and between October and December, especially after rain. To stay safe, consider wearing protective swimwear. Check local advisories or opt for swimming in hotel pools during peak jellyfish season. Lancawi is predominantly Muslim, so dress modestly, especially when visiting local villages or mosques. Alcohol is widely available thanks to Lancawi Island's duty-free status, but enjoy it respectfully, especially outside tourist areas. With these tips in mind, you are ready to immerse yourself in Lancawi's best attractions and vibrant culture. And there you have it. 14 unforgettable attractions, four traditional dishes, and five essential travel tips for your adventure in Lancawi. For more inspiration on Malaysia, check out our Paneang travel guide featuring 15 must-see attractions on the island and our travel guide to Malaysia's vibrant capital, Koala Lumpur, packed with 12 top things to do and three exciting day trips. Don't forget to like and subscribe. See you in the next video. Happy travels. [Music]
